text: Hello. This will be a discussion of a robotic preperitoneal ventral hernia repair. This is my preferred approach for smaller ventral hernias.
Three 8mm trocars are placed in the left lateral abdomen, and the robotic system is docked. The hernia is visualized at the umbilicus.

The surgeon initiates dissection into the preperitoneal space, starting cephalad and progressing caudad. The dissection continues through the central preperitoneal fat pad towards the hernia sac located at the umbilicus.

In the vast majority of these cases, I prefer a preperitoneal approach as seen here. There are several advantages, including placement of the mesh within a preperitoneal space in which it is not in contact with the abdominal viscera, minimizing the postoperative adhesions or risk of erosion. And this also allows a mesh which is not dual-sided to be utilized.

The surgeon reduces the hernia sac at the umbilicus, carefully dissecting it away from the overlying umbilical stalk. The preperitoneal approach allows for placement of the mesh within the preperitoneal space, minimizing the need for anchoring.

The surgeon continues dissection toward the right lateral side of the abdominal wall, transitioning from the preperitoneal fat pad to the thin lateral peritoneal layer. Care is taken to maintain the integrity of this layer to ensure the mesh remains excluded from the abdominal viscera.

The surgeon completes the circumferential dissection around the hernia site, creating a large prepared pocket for the placement of a synthetic mesh. The pocket is designed to provide sufficient overlap to minimize the risk of hernia recurrence.

The surgeon introduces a fascial closure suture and uses a ruler to measure the hernia defect, which is approximately 2.5 centimeters wide and 2 centimeters long. The ruler is removed, and the fascia is closed using a PDS absorbable unidirectional barbed suture, maintaining tension during closure.

The surgeon removes the fascial closure suture and introduces sutures for anchoring the mesh and closing the peritoneum. A polyester ProGrip mesh, cut to a thirteen-centimeter diameter circle, is introduced to provide wide overlap over the hernia defect, minimizing the chance of recurrence.

The surgeon anchors the polyester ProGrip mesh at the three o'clock, six o'clock, nine o'clock, and twelve o'clock positions using vicryl sutures, with an additional suture placed in the center of the mesh. This anchoring technique contrasts with intra-abdominal mesh placement, which typically requires circumferential suturing or tacking.
The surgeon uses a polyester ProGrip mesh for the hernia repair, which is visible as a synthetic, permanent mesh. A mid-weight polypropylene mesh is also frequently used in similar procedures.

The surgeon closes the peritoneal flap using a unidirectional barbed suture, ensuring complete exclusion of the mesh from the abdominal cavity. The peritoneum is inspected for holes, and any found are closed with interrupted vicryl sutures to prevent mesh contact with abdominal viscera.
The surgeon places the omentum back over the abdominal viscera, ensuring coverage. All needles and sutures are then carefully removed from the abdominal cavity.
